title: Fixed vs Variable Expenses
skillName: Planning with fixed and variable costs
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: Which of your current expenses are truly fixed and which ones are flexible enough to change if income drops?
How could separating fixed and variable expenses help you build a safer monthly budget?

stages.prompt: Which of these is usually a FIXED expense?

options.label: Monthly house rent under a contract
options.reflection: Correct. Rent under a contract is usually the same every month and hard to change quickly.

options.label: Grocery spending that changes week to week
options.reflection: Grocery is important, but the exact amount can often be adjusted, so it is more variable than fixed.

options.label: Mobile data recharges that you choose
options.reflection: You can usually switch plans or reduce usage, so this is more of a variable cost.

options.label: Eating out when you feel like it
options.reflection: Restaurant spending is highly variable and fully under your control, not fixed.

stages.prompt: Why is it useful to separate fixed and variable expenses in your budget?

options.label: There is no real benefit, it is just theory
options.reflection: In reality, knowing which costs are fixed helps you see how much of your income is already committed every month.

options.label: It shows which expenses you can adjust when income falls
options.reflection: Exactly. When you face money pressure, you can cut or reduce variable expenses first instead of missing fixed payments.

options.label: So that you can take more loans confidently
options.reflection: The goal is safety, not taking extra loans. Understanding expenses helps you avoid unnecessary borrowing.

options.label: To make budgeting more complicated
options.reflection: A simple fixed vs variable list actually makes budgeting clearer, not more confusing.
